metadata.title: Nutrition Optimization — Facts Database
sources: Layman DK, Frontiers in Nutrition, 2024 (cited by 31)
Wilkinson et al., Physiological Reports, 2023 (cited by 55)
Lak et al., Frontiers in Nutrition, 2024 (RCT, cited by 17)
Vieira et al., IJSNEM, 2025 (RCT, cited by 6)
Vieira et al., J Bodywork Mov Ther, 2025 (meta-analysis)
Simon et al., Alcohol Research: Current Reviews, 2023 (96 papers reviewed)
Bourgeois et al., Handbook of Substance Misuse, 2022
Meza et al., Alcohol and Alcoholism, 2022 (cited by 151)
Wachsmuth et al., Nutrients, 2022 (RCT, cited by 54)
Roth et al., European Journal of Applied Physiology, 2022 (cited by 33)
metadata.purpose: Evidence-based nutrition optimization for 130 kg beginner recomp in Ghaziabad, India

facts.finding: Older adults (>60y) require ≥2.8g leucine (~30g protein) per meal to stimulate MPS. Younger adults (<30y) show near-linear MPS response to protein content with no strict leucine threshold.
facts.application: At 28y, total per-meal protein (30-40g) is sufficient. No need to obsess over leucine thresholds.

facts.finding: Post-meal MPS anabolic response lasts 2-3 hours. Single large protein dinner is suboptimal. 4-5 meals spaced 3-4h apart maximizes total daily MPS.
facts.application: 4-5 meals of 35-45g protein each, spaced 3-4h apart.

facts.finding: Total daily protein intake is the most important dietary factor for lean body mass. At ≥1.6 g/kg/day, distribution and quality matter less.
facts.application: Hit ≥160g/day consistently first, then optimize distribution.

facts.finding: Dose-dependent association between dietary leucine and post-exercise MPS. Relationship plateaus at higher doses.
facts.application: Ensure 2-3g leucine per major meal (~30-40g quality protein).

facts.finding: Plant-based diets can supply enough protein and leucine to maximize hypertrophy if ~2g leucine/meal threshold is met through combining.
facts.application: Soya chunks + paneer + Greek yogurt for vegetarian days.

facts.finding: Balanced protein distribution (4-5 evenly spaced feedings of 30-40g) maximizes post-prandial MPS vs skewed distribution.
facts.source: Lak et al., Frontiers in Nutrition, 2024
facts.application: Breakfast 40g, Lunch 40g, Snack 30g, Dinner 50g, Pre-bed 30g = ~190g.

facts.finding: Protein quality matters less at higher total intakes (≥1.6g/kg/day). Vegan and omnivorous high-protein diets produce comparable myofibrillar protein synthesis.
facts.source: Layman DK, 2024; Monteyne et al., J Nutrition, 2023
facts.application: Vary protein sources across the day; don't stress about combining at every meal.

facts.finding: The 30-minute 'anabolic window' is a myth. Meta-analysis shows protein timing around exercise does not influence adaptations when total daily protein is matched.
facts.application: Don't stress about immediate post-workout shakes. Hit daily protein.

facts.finding: 8-week RCT: protein immediately before/after vs 3h before/after = no significant difference in gains. Both groups improved equally.
facts.source: Lak et al., Frontiers in Nutrition, 2024
facts.application: Train and eat when convenient.

facts.finding: Prior meta-analyses suggesting timing matters were limited by inactive comparator designs (control got no protein at all).
facts.source: Lak et al., 2024; Schoenfeld et al., 2013
facts.application: Supplement industry timing claims are based on flawed studies.

facts.finding: Anabolic window is wider (~4-6h) than thought. Modest benefit to protein within hours post-training, especially if fasted or pre-workout meal was >4h prior.
facts.source: Deane et al., Frontiers in Nutrition, 2024
facts.application: If training fasted, eat within 1-2h post-workout.

facts.finding: Daily protein threshold for maximizing hypertrophy: ~1.62 g/kg bodyweight/day. Beyond this, diminishing returns.
facts.application: Target 1.6g/kg of lean/ideal mass = ~180-200g for Achyut.

facts.finding: No direct meta-analysis evidence that carb cycling is superior to consistent caloric intake for body recomposition. Supported by tradition, not RCTs.
facts.application: For beginners, consistent moderate deficit is simpler and equally effective.

facts.finding: High-carb diets better maintain exercise performance vs low-carb, but body composition changes are similar when protein/calories matched.
facts.application: Keep carbs moderate-to-high on training days for performance.

facts.finding: Multi-component programs (IF + carb cycling + exercise) improve body composition, but unclear which component drives results.
facts.application: Overall approach works; carb cycling specifically may not add value.

facts.finding: Key factors for lean mass sparing during caloric restriction: protein ≥1.6g/kg, resistance training volume, moderate deficit. Carb manipulation is secondary.
facts.application: Focus on protein + training + moderate deficit, not carb timing.

facts.finding: True recomposition (simultaneous fat loss + lean gain) is achievable in beginners and overweight individuals. Carb cycling not required.
facts.application: As 130 kg beginner, recomposition is very achievable with fundamentals.

facts.finding: Sodium sweat loss: 500-2000mg/hour depending on intensity and heat. At 1L/hour sweat rate, ~2% total body sodium lost per hour.
facts.application: 300-500mg sodium pre-workout for sessions >60 min.

facts.finding: Pre-workout sodium loading (500-1000mg in 500-750ml water, 60-90 min pre) enhances plasma volume and pump.
facts.source: Blow et al., 2025; ISSN position stands
facts.application: 1/4 tsp salt in 500ml water before training, especially in Ghaziabad heat.

facts.finding: Exercise-associated muscle cramping is primarily from muscle fatigue/neuromuscular issues, NOT dehydration/electrolyte imbalance (though sodium can contribute in prolonged heat exercise).
facts.application: Address cramping with warm-up and technique, not just salt.

facts.finding: Co-transport of water + sodium enhances hydration more than plain water during exercise.
facts.application: Add salt + glucose to water for long sessions (ORS principle).

facts.finding: ISSN recommends 300-600mg sodium/hour for exercise >1 hour. For <60 min sessions, normal dietary sodium is sufficient.
facts.application: Pre-workout salt optional for 45-60 min lifting. Indian food is well-salted.

facts.finding: Meta-analysis: fasted vs fed resistance training produces similar gains in fat-free mass, hypertrophy, and strength when total daily nutrition matched.
facts.source: Vieira et al., J Bodywork Mov Ther, 2025
facts.application: Fasted training is fine if mornings work best.

facts.finding: RCT: fasted vs fed resistance training = similar hypertrophy and strength gains. Fasted did NOT impair muscle growth.
facts.application: Train when consistent. Eat protein-rich meal after.

facts.finding: Ramadan fasting study: both fasted and fed groups maintained strength and hypertrophy.
facts.application: Training during fasting is safe and effective.

facts.finding: Exercise enhances anabolic sensitivity to protein feeding for up to 24h. Fasted training has modest advantage to fed state for acute MPS.
facts.application: If training fasted, eat high-protein meal within 1-2h post-workout.

facts.finding: Both acute and chronic alcohol decrease MPS and increase protein degradation via mTORC1 pathway inhibition.
facts.source: Simon et al., Alcohol Research: Current Reviews, 2023
facts.application: Minimize or eliminate alcohol during serious training phases.

facts.finding: Single binge (BAC ≥0.08, ~4-5 drinks) can cause acute myopathy and impairs MPS for 24h+.
facts.application: Binge drinking post-workout is worst-case for muscle growth.

facts.finding: 40-60% of chronic heavy drinkers develop alcohol-related myopathy. Chronic use increases breakdown via UPP/atrogenes.
facts.source: Simon et al., 2023; Meza et al., 2022
facts.application: Regular heavy drinking (3-4+ drinks/day) undermines all fitness goals.

facts.finding: Alcohol impairs muscle mitochondrial function, reducing ATP production and training performance.
facts.source: Simon et al., 2023; Dominguez et al., 2024
facts.application: Avoid alcohol 24h before heavy training sessions.

facts.finding: Post-exercise alcohol (1.0g ethanol/kg, ~5-6 drinks) significantly delays strength recovery. Even 0.5g/kg may impair recovery.
facts.application: Keep to 1-2 drinks max if socializing post-workout.

facts.finding: Abstinence improves muscle mass/function, but muscles are NOT fully restored to normal — some damage irreversible.
facts.application: The sooner you cut back, the better.

facts.finding: Alcohol's effect on MPS is dose-dependent: 1-2 drinks minimal impact, ≥0.5g/kg causes significant MPS suppression (24-37% reduction).
facts.source: Bourgeois et al., 2022; Simon et al., 2023
facts.application: 1-2 occasional drinks with protein is fine. Binge drinking is the problem.

facts.finding: Soya chunks: highest protein-per-rupee in India. ~52g protein/100g dry. Cost: ~₹0.15-0.20/g protein.
facts.application: 50-100g soya chunks daily as protein base. 100g dry = 52g protein for ~₹10-15.

facts.finding: Whey protein concentrate: ₹1.5-2.5/g protein (Indian brands). 30g scoop = ~24g protein.
facts.application: 2 scoops/day (48g protein) costs ~₹100-120/day.

facts.finding: Paneer: ~18g protein + ~20g fat/100g = 260+ kcal. NOT cost-effective for protein during recomp. Low-fat paneer/Greek yogurt better.
facts.application: Use paneer for flavor, not primary protein. Greek yogurt (hung curd) = 10g protein/100g at 60 kcal.

facts.finding: Eggs: ~6g protein each at ₹0.8-1.0/g protein (₹7-8/egg). 6-egg omelette = 36g protein for ~₹42-48.
facts.application: 4-6 whole eggs for breakfast with bread and vegetables.

facts.finding: Chicken breast: ~31g protein, 3.6g fat/100g (165 kcal). Cost: ~₹1.5-2.0/g protein at ₹250-300/kg.
facts.application: 250g chicken breast = 77g protein for 412 kcal. Ideal dinner on training days.

facts.finding: Indian breakfast foods (poha, upma, idli) are traditionally low protein (5-8g/serving). Modifications can reach 25-35g.
facts.application: High-protein poha: add 50g soya chunks + whey. High-protein cheela: 100g besan + 2 eggs.

facts.finding: Vegetarian Indian diet can achieve 180g+ protein/day under 2,200 kcal using whey, soya chunks, Greek yogurt, and strategic food choices.
facts.application: Cost: ~₹200-250/day fully vegetarian.

facts.finding: Lentils/legumes: poor protein-per-calorie. 200g cooked dal = only 14-18g protein for 230+ kcal. Not primary protein sources during recomp.
facts.application: Eat dal for fiber/micronutrients, not as main protein source.

facts.finding: Hierarchy for recomposition: 1) Progressive resistance training, 2) Total protein ≥1.6g/kg, 3) Slight caloric deficit, 4) Sleep 7-9h, 5) Protein distribution, 6) Nutrient timing, 7) Carb cycling, 8) Supplements.
facts.source: Synthesized from Roth 2022, Morton 2018, Layman 2024, Lak 2024
facts.application: Don't optimize #6-8 until #1-4 are consistent for weeks.

facts.finding: ISSN recommends 20-40g protein per meal, 4-5x daily, spaced 3-4h, total 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day.
facts.source: Jäger et al., JISSN Position Stand, 2017
facts.application: Master template: 4 meals × 40g + 1 snack × 20g = 180g/day.

facts.finding: Protein + carb together post-exercise does not enhance MPS beyond protein alone when daily totals matched.
facts.application: Don't worry about specific carb:protein ratio post-workout.

facts.finding: Creatine monohydrate (3-5g/day) is the most evidence-backed supplement for lean mass and strength.
facts.source: ISSN position stands; hundreds of RCTs
facts.application: 5g daily, any time. No loading needed. ~₹300-500/month in India.

facts.finding: Caffeine (3-6mg/kg, 30-60 min pre-workout) reliably improves resistance training performance.
facts.application: Strong black coffee 45 min before training. Skip if training late evening.

facts.finding: Sleep deprivation (≤5.5h) reduces MPS, increases cortisol, and impairs recovery. Even one night of poor sleep can reduce performance 5-10%.
facts.application: 7-9h sleep is non-negotiable for recomp.
